annotations.label: Metric space

anchor.snippet: Formally, a metric space is an ordered pair

annotations.note: `MetricSpace` is the standard class extending `PseudoMetricSpace` with the indiscernibles axiom.

annotations.label: Distance from a point to itself is zero

anchor.snippet: The distance from a point to itself is zero

annotations.note: `dist_self : dist x x = 0` is exactly this axiom.

annotations.label: Positivity axiom

anchor.snippet: (Positivity) The distance between two distinct points is always positive

annotations.note: `dist_pos : 0 < dist x y ↔ x ≠ y` directly states positivity for distinct points.

annotations.label: Symmetry axiom

anchor.snippet: the distance from x to y is always the same as the distance from y to x

annotations.note: `dist_comm` is the symmetry axiom of (pseudo)metric spaces.

annotations.label: Triangle inequality axiom

anchor.snippet: The triangle inequality holds

annotations.note: `dist_triangle x y z : dist x z ≤ dist x y + dist y z` is the standard triangle inequality.

annotations.label: Hamming distance

anchor.snippet: the Hamming distance , which measures the number of characters that need to be changed

annotations.note: `hammingDist x y` counts positions where finite tuples differ.

annotations.label: Real line as metric space

anchor.snippet: The real numbers with the distance function

annotations.note: The instance `Real.metricSpace` (with `Real.dist_eq : dist x y = |x - y|`) provides the standard metric on ℝ.

annotations.label: Euclidean distance

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean distance familiar from school mathematics

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace 𝕜 n` with `dist x y = √(∑ i, dist (x i) (y i) ^ 2)` formalizes the Euclidean distance.

annotations.label: Taxicab/Manhattan distance

anchor.snippet: The taxicab or Manhattan distance is defined by

annotations.note: `WithLp 1 (α × β)` carries the L¹/Manhattan distance with `WithLp.prod_dist_eq_of_L1 : dist x y = dist x.fst y.fst + dist x.snd y.snd`.

annotations.label: Chebyshev distance

anchor.snippet: or Chebyshev distance is defined by

annotations.note: `PiLp ⊤` / `WithLp ⊤` provides the sup (Chebyshev/L∞) metric on finite products.

annotations.label: Equivalence of Euclidean, taxicab, Chebyshev metrics

anchor.snippet: This observation can be quantified with the formula

annotations.note: Mathlib has individual `PiLp.norm_eq_*` lemmas relating L^p norms but no single statement quantifying the Lᵖ equivalence as in the article.

annotations.label: Discrete metric

anchor.snippet: In this discrete metric , all distinct points are 1 unit apart

annotations.note: Mathlib has `DiscreteTopology` but no canonical `MetricSpace` instance providing the 0/1 discrete metric on an arbitrary type.

annotations.label: Induced metric on a subspace

anchor.snippet: the induced metric on A is a function

annotations.note: `MetricSpace.induced` pulls back a metric along an injection, giving the induced subspace metric.

annotations.label: Open ball

anchor.section: The topology of a metric space
anchor.snippet: the open ball of radius r around x is defined to be the set of points that are strictly less than distance r from x

annotations.note: `Metric.ball x ε` is defined as `{y | dist y x < ε}`.

annotations.label: Neighborhood

anchor.section: The topology of a metric space
anchor.snippet: is a neighborhood of x (informally, it contains all points "close enough" to x )

annotations.note: `Metric.mem_nhds_iff : s ∈ 𝓝 x ↔ ∃ ε > 0, ball x ε ⊆ s` characterizes metric neighborhoods.

annotations.label: Open set

anchor.section: The topology of a metric space
anchor.snippet: An open set is a set which is a neighborhood of all its points

annotations.note: `Metric.isOpen_iff : IsOpen s ↔ ∀ x ∈ s, ∃ ε > 0, ball x ε ⊆ s`.

annotations.label: Open balls form a base for a topology

anchor.section: The topology of a metric space
anchor.snippet: It follows that the open balls form a base for a topology on M

annotations.note: `Metric.nhds_basis_ball` shows open balls form a neighborhood basis (hence a base for the topology).

annotations.label: Metrizable space

anchor.section: The topology of a metric space
anchor.snippet: Topological spaces which are compatible with a metric are called metrizable

annotations.note: `MetrizableSpace` encodes metrizability of a topological space.

annotations.label: Nagata–Smirnov metrization theorem

anchor.section: The topology of a metric space
anchor.snippet: The Nagata–Smirnov metrization theorem gives a characterization of metrizability

annotations.note: Grep for `NagataSmirnov` returns no matches; only Urysohn's metrization theorem is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convergence in Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: A sequence ( x n ) converges to a point x if for every ε > 0 there is an integer N such that for all n > N

annotations.note: Convergence is characterized via `Metric.tendsto_atTop` in ε–N form.

annotations.label: Convergence in a topological space

anchor.snippet: A sequence ( x n ) converges to a point x if for every open set U containing x

annotations.note: `Tendsto u atTop (𝓝 x)` is the standard topological notion of sequential convergence.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence

anchor.snippet: a sequence ( x n ) in a metric space M is Cauchy if for every ε > 0

annotations.note: `CauchySeq` is defined on uniform spaces and characterized via `Metric.cauchySeq_iff` on metric spaces.

annotations.label: Convergent sequences are Cauchy

anchor.snippet: By the triangle inequality, any convergent sequence is Cauchy

annotations.note: `Filter.Tendsto.cauchySeq` shows a convergent sequence is Cauchy.

annotations.label: Complete metric space

anchor.snippet: every Cauchy sequence in M converges—then M is complete

annotations.note: `CompleteSpace` is the standard class; on metric spaces it agrees with every Cauchy sequence converging.

annotations.label: Completion

anchor.snippet: every metric space has a unique completion , which is a complete space that contains the given space as a dense subset

annotations.note: `UniformSpace.Completion` is defined for uniform spaces and inherits a `MetricSpace` instance for metric inputs.

annotations.label: p-adic numbers as completion of the rationals

anchor.snippet: the p -adic numbers are defined as the completion of the rationals under a different metric

annotations.note: `Padic p` is constructed in Mathlib as the Cauchy-sequence completion of ℚ under the p-adic metric.

annotations.label: Bounded metric space

anchor.snippet: A metric space M is bounded if there is an r such that no pair of points in M is more than distance r apart

annotations.note: `Bornology.IsBounded` with `Metric.isBounded_iff_subset_closedBall` characterizes bounded sets in metric spaces.

annotations.label: Diameter

anchor.snippet: The least such r is called the diameter of M

annotations.note: `Metric.diam` (and `EMetric.diam`) define the diameter of a set.

annotations.label: Totally bounded space

anchor.snippet: The space M is called precompact or totally bounded if for every r > 0 there is a finite cover

annotations.note: `TotallyBounded` is defined for uniform spaces; `EMetric.totallyBounded_iff` gives the ε-net characterization.

annotations.label: Totally bounded implies bounded

anchor.snippet: Every totally bounded space is bounded

annotations.note: `TotallyBounded.isBounded` is exactly this implication.

annotations.label: Compact metric space (equivalent definitions)

anchor.snippet: There are several equivalent definitions of compactness in metric spaces

annotations.note: `isCompact_iff_totallyBounded_isComplete` gives the standard equivalence; sequential-compactness equivalences also exist for metric spaces.

annotations.label: Closed interval is compact

anchor.snippet: One example of a compact space is the closed interval

annotations.note: `isCompact_Icc` (exported from `CompactIccSpace`) shows closed intervals in linearly-ordered topological spaces are compact.

annotations.label: Lebesgue's number lemma

anchor.snippet: Lebesgue's number lemma , which shows that for any open cover of a compact space

annotations.note: `lebesgue_number_lemma` states exactly this result.

annotations.label: Distance-preserving function

anchor.snippet: is distance-preserving

annotations.note: `Isometry f` is defined as `∀ x y, edist (f x) (f y) = edist x y`.

annotations.label: Distance-preserving functions are injective

anchor.snippet: It follows from the metric space axioms that a distance-preserving function is injective

annotations.note: `Isometry.injective` directly proves this on emetric spaces.

annotations.label: Isometry

anchor.snippet: A bijective distance-preserving function is called an isometry

annotations.note: `IsometryEquiv` (`α ≃ᵢ β`) is the bundled bijective isometry.

annotations.label: Isometric spaces

anchor.snippet: If there is an isometry between the spaces M 1 and M 2 , they are said to be isometric

annotations.note: Existence of an `IsometryEquiv` between spaces is Mathlib's way of saying they are isometric.

annotations.label: Continuous map (topological)

anchor.snippet: Topological definition

annotations.note: `Continuous` is the standard topological definition (preimages of opens are open).

annotations.label: Sequential continuity

anchor.snippet: Sequential continuity

annotations.note: `SeqContinuous f` is the sequential-continuity predicate.

annotations.label: ε–δ continuity

anchor.snippet: ε–δ definition

annotations.note: `Metric.continuous_iff` is the ε–δ characterization of continuity for metric spaces.

annotations.label: Homeomorphism

anchor.snippet: A homeomorphism is a continuous bijection whose inverse is also continuous

annotations.note: `Homeomorph` (`α ≃ₜ β`) is the bundled continuous bijection with continuous inverse.

annotations.label: Uniformly continuous map

anchor.snippet: is uniformly continuous if for every real number ε > 0 there exists δ > 0

annotations.note: `UniformContinuous` is defined on uniform spaces; `Metric.uniformContinuous_iff` gives the ε–δ form.

annotations.label: Uniformly continuous maps preserve Cauchy sequences

anchor.snippet: uniformly continuous maps take Cauchy sequences in M 1 to Cauchy sequences

annotations.note: `UniformContinuous.comp_cauchySeq` gives the preservation of Cauchy sequences.

annotations.label: Heine–Cantor theorem

anchor.snippet: the Heine–Cantor theorem states that if M 1 is compact, then every continuous map is uniformly continuous

annotations.note: `CompactSpace.uniformContinuous_of_continuous` is exactly Heine–Cantor.

annotations.label: Lipschitz map

anchor.snippet: A Lipschitz map is one that stretches distances by at most a bounded factor

annotations.note: `LipschitzWith K f` (with `lipschitzWith_iff_dist_le_mul`) formalizes Lipschitz maps.

annotations.label: Rectifiable iff has Lipschitz reparametrization

anchor.snippet: a curve in a metric space is rectifiable (has finite length) if and only if it has a Lipschitz reparametrization

annotations.note: Mathlib has `eVariationOn`/`BoundedVariationOn`, but no statement equating finite length with admitting a Lipschitz reparametrization.

annotations.label: Metric map (nonexpanding)

anchor.snippet: A 1-Lipschitz map is sometimes called a nonexpanding or metric map

annotations.note: Nonexpanding maps are `LipschitzWith 1 f`; no dedicated `NonexpandingMap` notion exists.

annotations.label: Contraction

anchor.snippet: A K -Lipschitz map for K < 1 is called a contraction

annotations.note: `ContractingWith K f := K < 1 ∧ LipschitzWith K f`.

annotations.label: Banach fixed-point theorem

anchor.snippet: The Banach fixed-point theorem states that if M is a complete metric space

annotations.note: `ContractingWith.exists_fixedPoint` together with `fixedPoint_unique` formalize Banach's fixed-point theorem.

annotations.label: Quasi-isometric embedding

anchor.snippet: is a quasi-isometric embedding if there exist constants A ≥ 1 and B ≥ 0

annotations.note: Grep for `QuasiIsometry` returns no matches in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quasi-isometry

anchor.snippet: It is a quasi-isometry if in addition it is quasi-surjective

annotations.note: No `QuasiIsometry` definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Švarc–Milnor lemma

anchor.snippet: the Švarc–Milnor lemma states that all spaces on which a group acts geometrically are quasi-isometric

annotations.note: No file mentions Švarc–Milnor; the prerequisite quasi-isometry notion is absent.

annotations.label: Topologically equivalent spaces

anchor.snippet: are said to be topologically equivalent

annotations.note: Topological equivalence is existence of a `Homeomorph` between the spaces.

annotations.label: Uniformic spaces

anchor.snippet: They are called uniformic (uniformly isomorphic) if there is a uniform isomorphism

annotations.note: `UniformEquiv` (`α ≃ᵤ β`) bundles uniform isomorphisms.

annotations.label: Bilipschitz homeomorphic

anchor.snippet: They are called bilipschitz homeomorphic if there is a bilipschitz bijection

annotations.note: Mathlib expresses bilipschitz equivalence via `LipschitzWith` on both directions; no dedicated bundled `Bilipschitz` structure.

annotations.label: Quasi-isometric spaces

anchor.snippet: They are called quasi-isometric if there is a quasi-isometry between them

annotations.note: Lacks the underlying quasi-isometry definition.

annotations.label: Metric induced by a norm

anchor.snippet: Any normed vector space can be equipped with a metric in which the distance between two vectors x and y is given by

annotations.note: `dist_eq_norm : dist x y = ‖x - y‖` together with the `SeminormedAddCommGroup` metric formalize this.

annotations.label: Translation-invariant and homogeneous metric comes from a norm

anchor.snippet: then

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement constructs a norm from a translation-invariant, homogeneous metric on a vector space.

annotations.label: Kuratowski embedding

anchor.snippet: the Kuratowski embedding allows one to see any metric space as a subspace of a normed vector space

annotations.note: `kuratowskiEmbedding` isometrically embeds any separable metric space into `ℓ∞(ℕ, ℝ)`.

annotations.label: Banach space

anchor.snippet: a complete normed vector space is known as a Banach space

annotations.note: Banach spaces are encoded as `[NormedAddCommGroup E] [NormedSpace 𝕜 E] [CompleteSpace E]`; no separate `Banach` class.

annotations.label: Linear maps continuous iff Lipschitz

anchor.snippet: linear transformations between them are continuous if and only if they are Lipschitz

annotations.note: `continuous_of_linear_of_bound` together with `ContinuousLinearMap.lipschitz` formalize that a linear map is continuous iff Lipschitz.

annotations.label: Curve and its length

anchor.snippet: A curve in a metric space ( M , d ) is a continuous function

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `eVariationOn` (and `Path`) for length-style notions; there is no single `Curve.length` definition matching the article verbatim.

annotations.label: Rectifiable curve

anchor.snippet: a curve of finite length is called rectifiable

annotations.note: `BoundedVariationOn` captures finite-length curves but no `Rectifiable` name appears.

annotations.label: Geodesic

anchor.snippet: After reparametrization by arc length

annotations.note: Riemannian-manifold geodesics appear in Mathlib, but no general metric-space `Geodesic` definition exists.

annotations.label: Geodesic metric space

anchor.snippet: A geodesic metric space is a metric space which admits a geodesic between any two of its points

annotations.note: No `GeodesicSpace` class found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Length space (intrinsic metric)

anchor.snippet: The space M is a length space (or the metric d is intrinsic )

annotations.note: No `LengthSpace` class found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Punctured plane is length but not geodesic

anchor.snippet: An example of a length space which is not geodesic is the Euclidean plane minus the origin

annotations.note: Requires length/geodesic-space framework which is absent.

annotations.label: Sphere with straight-line metric not a length space

anchor.snippet: An example of a metric space which is not a length space is given by the straight-line metric on the sphere

annotations.note: Requires length-space framework which is absent.

annotations.label: Intrinsic distance function

anchor.snippet: one can define a new, intrinsic distance function

annotations.note: No general construction of an intrinsic metric from a metric appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riemannian manifold and length of a path

anchor.snippet: A Riemannian manifold is a space equipped with a Riemannian metric tensor

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Mathlib.Geometry.Manifold.Riemannian` develops Riemannian manifolds (built on `IsManifold`) with a Riemannian metric and `PathELength` for path length.

annotations.label: CAT(k) characterization of sectional curvature bound

anchor.snippet: a Riemannian manifold is a CAT( k ) space

annotations.note: No CAT(k) spaces are defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metric measure space

anchor.snippet: a metric measure space is a metric space equipped with a Borel regular measure such that every ball has positive measure

annotations.note: No dedicated `MetricMeasureSpace` class found; one uses `[MetricSpace X] [MeasureSpace X]` ad hoc.

annotations.label: Discrete metric space

anchor.snippet: A metric space is discrete if its induced topology is the discrete topology

annotations.note: `DiscreteTopology` together with a `MetricSpace` instance expresses this; `DiscreteTopology.of_forall_le_dist` characterizes it via separated points.

annotations.label: Graph (shortest-path) metric

anchor.snippet: the set V of vertices of G can be turned into a metric space by defining the distance between vertices x and y to be the length of the shortest edge path

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.dist` defines the graph (shortest-path) distance, but Mathlib does not register a `MetricSpace` instance on the vertex set.

annotations.label: Word metric

anchor.snippet: this construction is applied to the Cayley graph of a (typically infinite) finitely-generated group , yielding the word metric

annotations.note: No `wordMetric` and no Cayley-graph metric construction in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probabilistic embedding into tree metric

anchor.snippet: any finite metric space can be probabilistically embedded into a tree metric

annotations.note: No probabilistic tree-embedding theorem found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniform (supremum) metric on bounded functions

anchor.snippet: This metric is called the uniform metric or supremum metric

annotations.note: `BoundedContinuousFunction` (`α →ᵇ β`) carries the sup metric `dist f g = ⨆ x, dist (f x) (g x)`.

annotations.label: Completeness of bounded function space

anchor.snippet: If M is complete, then this function space is complete as well

annotations.note: `instance instCompleteSpace [CompleteSpace β] : CompleteSpace (α →ᵇ β)` formalizes this.

annotations.label: Levenshtein distance

anchor.snippet: while the Levenshtein distance measures the minimal number of deletions, insertions, and substitutions

annotations.note: No `Levenshtein`/edit-distance file exists in Mathlib (grep for `levenshtein`/`EditDistance` returns no matches).

annotations.label: Distance from a point to a set

anchor.snippet: The distance from S to a point x of M is, informally, the distance from x to the closest point of S

annotations.note: `Metric.infDist` (and `Metric.infEDist`) define the distance from a point to a set.

annotations.label: Distance zero iff in closure

anchor.snippet: if and only if x belongs to the closure of S

annotations.note: Combined with `infEDist_eq_zero_iff_mem_closure`, gives `infDist x s = 0 ↔ x ∈ closure s`.

annotations.label: Continuity of distance-to-set

anchor.snippet: and therefore the map

annotations.note: `continuous_infDist_pt` shows `x ↦ infDist x s` is continuous.

annotations.label: Metric spaces are completely regular

anchor.snippet: Incidentally, this shows that metric spaces are completely regular

annotations.note: `CompletelyRegularSpace` exists, and metric spaces being T3.5 follows from being normal, but no explicit `instance CompletelyRegularSpace` for metric spaces was found.

annotations.label: Hausdorff distance

anchor.snippet: Given two subsets S and T of M , their Hausdorff distance is

annotations.note: `Metric.hausdorffEDist` (and `Metric.hausdorffDist`) define the Hausdorff distance between subsets.

annotations.label: Hausdorff distance is a metric on compact subsets

anchor.snippet: Thus the Hausdorff distance defines a metric on the set of compact subsets of M

annotations.note: `NonemptyCompacts.instMetricSpace` endows nonempty compact subsets with the Hausdorff metric.

annotations.label: Gromov–Hausdorff distance

anchor.snippet: The Gromov–Hausdorff metric defines a distance between (isometry classes of) compact metric spaces

annotations.note: `GromovHausdorff.GHSpace` and its metric instance formalize the Gromov–Hausdorff distance.

annotations.label: Snowflake of a metric

anchor.snippet: Given a metric space ( X , d ) and an increasing concave function

annotations.note: `Snowflaking` formalizes the power-of-d snowflake; the general concave-f version is not stated.

annotations.label: Tight span

anchor.snippet: The tight span of a metric space is another metric space

annotations.note: No tight-span / injective-hull construction in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Knight's move metric

anchor.snippet: The knight's move metric , the minimal number of knight's moves to reach one point

annotations.note: No knight's-move metric appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: British Rail metric

anchor.snippet: The British Rail metric (also called the "post office metric" or the " French railway metric ")

annotations.note: No British-Rail/SNCF metric is constructed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Product metric

anchor.snippet: the product metric is defined by

annotations.note: `Prod.metricSpaceMax` puts the sup product metric on `α × β`.

annotations.label: Countable product metric

anchor.snippet: a metric on the topological product of countably many metric spaces can be obtained

annotations.note: `PiCountable.metricSpace` constructs a (non-instance) metric on countable products `∀ i, F i` of metric spaces.

annotations.label: Uncountable products need not be metrizable

anchor.snippet: The topological product of uncountably many metric spaces need not be metrizable

annotations.note: No explicit non-metrizability result for uncountable products in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quotient pseudometric

anchor.snippet: then we can endow the quotient set

annotations.note: `Mathlib.Topology.MetricSpace.Gluing` constructs quotient (pseudo)metric spaces in the gluing context, but no general `Quotient.pseudoMetric` of a setoid is defined.

annotations.label: Universal property of quotient metric

anchor.snippet: The quotient metric

annotations.note: No general universal property of a metric-space quotient is stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sequential iff quotient of a metric space

anchor.snippet: A topological space is sequential if and only if it is a (topological) quotient of a metric space

annotations.note: `SequentialSpace` exists but no characterization as quotients of metric spaces was found.

annotations.label: Extended metric

anchor.snippet: Such a function is also called an extended metric

annotations.note: `EMetricSpace` (and `PseudoEMetricSpace`) formalize extended metrics taking values in `ℝ≥0∞`.

annotations.label: Extended metric replaceable by topologically equivalent real-valued metric

anchor.snippet: Every extended metric can be replaced by a real-valued metric that is topologically equivalent

annotations.note: No general lemma in Mathlib converts an `EMetricSpace` to a topologically equivalent `MetricSpace`.

annotations.label: Pseudometric

anchor.snippet: A pseudometric on

annotations.note: `PseudoMetricSpace` is exactly a pseudometric structure.

annotations.label: Quasimetric

anchor.snippet: a quasimetric is defined as a function that satisfies all axioms for a metric with the possible exception of symmetry

annotations.note: No `QuasiMetric` class is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quasimetric on the reals

anchor.snippet: A quasimetric on the reals can be defined by setting

annotations.note: No quasimetric infrastructure in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metametric

anchor.snippet: In a metametric , all the axioms of a metric are satisfied except that the distance between identical points is not necessarily zero

annotations.note: No metametric/partial-metric class in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Semimetric

anchor.snippet: A semimetric on

annotations.note: No `Semimetric` notion (dropping the triangle inequality) is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: ρ-relaxed triangle and ρ-inframetric inequality

anchor.snippet: Some authors work with a weaker form of the triangle inequality

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize relaxed/inframetric triangle inequalities.

annotations.label: Implications between inframetric inequalities

anchor.snippet: The ρ-inframetric inequality implies the ρ-relaxed triangle inequality

annotations.note: Requires the absent inframetric definitions.

annotations.label: Premetric

anchor.snippet: Relaxing the last three axioms leads to the notion of a premetric

annotations.note: No `Premetric` class in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Premetric induces a topology

anchor.snippet: Any premetric gives rise to a topology as follows

annotations.note: No premetric framework in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Premetric space is sequential

anchor.snippet: Every premetric space is a topological space, and in fact a sequential space

annotations.note: No premetric framework in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pseudoquasimetric (hemimetric)

anchor.snippet: a pseudoquasimetric (sometimes called hemimetric ) relaxes both the indiscernibility axiom and the symmetry axiom

annotations.note: Mathlib has no pseudoquasimetric/hemimetric class.

annotations.label: Sierpiński space as pseudoquasimetric

anchor.snippet: A very basic example of a pseudoquasimetric space

annotations.note: Requires the absent pseudoquasimetric framework.

annotations.label: Metric on multisets

anchor.snippet: A function d on the set of nonempty finite multisets of elements of a set M is a metric

annotations.note: No metric structure on the type of finite multisets appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiset metric restricts to ordinary metric

anchor.snippet: every multiset metric yields an ordinary metric when restricted to sets of two elements

annotations.note: Requires the absent multiset-metric framework.
